metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: No `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` items found.
findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the docs/architecture.md row deletion, e.g. `grep -c '_processors_keys\|_processors_identity' docs/architecture.md` with an expected count, or `grep -v '_processors_keys' docs/architecture.md | grep -q '_processors_identity' && echo OK`.
evidence: 'its docs/architecture.md offenders-table row deleted' — stated as a requirement but no grep/awk command to confirm the row is absent post-change.
tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py covers allowlist/ceilings consistency but does not read docs/architecture.md.
findings.finding: AC4 requires deletion of the offenders-table row in docs/architecture.md but provides no concrete proving command to verify the row is gone after the edit.
scenarios: An agent removes the row from the allowlist files (covered by the contract test) but forgets to edit architecture.md, leaving a stale docs entry. No AC command catches this.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'Verified by: `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` (the stale row is absent).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC3, e.g. `python -c 'from rebar.reducer._processors import _fold_author_attribution, _rederive_keyring_keys, process_key_event, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, attestation_kind, process_signature'` verifies all 6 symbols remain importable from the original namespace.
evidence: 'The 6 named symbols live in the leaf and are re-exported (# noqa: F401) from _processors.py so rebar.reducer._processors still exposes them unchanged' — no grep or python -c command provided to verify this directly.

The test commands listed (test_attestations_reducer.py, test_signing.py, test_legacy_signature_mirror_drop.py) may exercise the symbols indirectly but do not constitute a direct, named proving command for the re-export requirement.
findings.finding: AC3 ('The 6 named symbols live in the leaf and are re-exported from _processors.py') has no concrete proving command. An agent could satisfy the LOC and lint checks while accidentally omitting a symbol or placing it only in one file, with the omission only caught incidentally by tests — if the relevant tests happen to exercise that import path.
scenarios: An agent moves 5 of 6 symbols, forgets _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, lint passes, LOC counts pass, but tests that don't import _bootstrap_genesis_keyring from _processors pass too — leaving a broken re-export undetected until a downstream consumer fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: 'Verified by: `python -c "from rebar.reducer._processors import _fold_author_attribution, _rederive_keyring_keys, process_key_event, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, attestation_kind, process_signature; print(\'OK\')"`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'true leaf / reference only each other' claim: note that _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind are also called by process_create, process_comment, process_revert, and process_snapshot (which stay in _processors.py), so the re-import lines in _processors.py are load-bearing for internal callers as well as external back-compat — not just cosmetic noqa shims.
evidence: 'These reference only each other (a true leaf): process_key_event calls _rederive_keyring_keys; process_signature calls attestation_kind and _fold_author_attribution'
_processors.py line 150: process_create calls _fold_author_attribution(state, event)
_processors.py line 394: process_comment calls _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event)
_processors.py line 520: process_revert calls _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event)
_processors.py line 213: process_create calls _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(...)
_processors.py line 852: process_snapshot calls _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(...)
_processors.py line 834: process_snapshot calls attestation_kind(...)
findings.finding: The plan describes the 6 moved symbols as 'a true leaf' that 'reference only each other', but _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind are also called by functions that STAY in _processors.py (process_create, process_comment, process_revert, process_snapshot). The re-import lines serve double-duty as both external back-compat re-exports AND load-bearing internal callers, but the plan's framing presents them as purely back-compat shims (# noqa: F401), which may cause an implementer to treat the re-imports as cosmetic and misunderstand why they are required.
findings.location: Plan body — 'MOVE (by name)' paragraph; 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' claim
scenarios: An implementer reading 'true leaf / reference only each other' concludes the re-export lines are purely for external consumers and marks them noqa without recognizing they also satisfy the same-module callers (process_create, process_comment, process_revert, process_snapshot); if the implementer instead reorganizes those internal call-sites differently (e.g., inline copies or a local private alias) the behavior would still be correct but diverge from the plan's intent.

A future reviewer auditing the re-export lines might remove them as 'unused re-exports' since the annotation says # noqa: F401 (suppress unused-import), not realizing they are needed by internal callers in _processors.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' with: 'Within the moved cluster, the inter-symbol calls are: process_key_event → _rederive_keyring_keys; process_signature → attestation_kind, _fold_author_attribution. Note: _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind are also called by process_create, process_comment, process_revert, and process_snapshot, which stay in _processors.py; the re-import lines (from ._processors_identity import ...) in _processors.py are therefore load-bearing for those internal callers as well as for external back-compat — not purely cosmetic # noqa: F401 shims.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pyproject.toml` (mutmut `only_mutate` — add `_processors_identity.py`) and `docs/mutation-testing.md` (module→test mapping table) to the plan's edit-target list, or explicitly mark mutation-testing config out-of-scope with a rationale.
evidence: pyproject.toml:432: 'src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py' in [tool.mutmut] only_mutate
docs/mutation-testing.md:55: '| `reducer/_processors.py` | `tests/scripts/reducer/`, `tests/interfaces/store/test_reducer_single_source.py` |'
ABSENCE: neither pyproject.toml nor docs/mutation-testing.md appears anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: pyproject.toml [tool.mutmut] `only_mutate` lists `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` but is not named as an edit target. After the split, the 6 moved symbols live in `_processors_identity.py` and silently fall out of mutation-testing scope; neither `pyproject.toml` nor `docs/mutation-testing.md` is mentioned in the plan.
findings.location: Plan body: acceptance criteria and MOVE section; pyproject.toml lines 432, 448-449
scenarios: After the split, process_key_event and process_signature (and the 4 private helpers) are mutated in _processors_identity.py but the mutmut config still only names _processors.py — so no mutants are generated for the moved code and the 99.4% mutation score silently refers only to the retained processors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that the `_processors_keys.py` row has been removed from `docs/architecture.md` (e.g., `grep -c '_processors_keys' docs/architecture.md` returns 0).
evidence: Plan body: 'The sub-floor _processors_keys.py row is stale/superseded and is DELETED by this ticket.'
AC: '_processors.py removed from .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt; its docs/architecture.md offenders-table row deleted.'
The AC phrase 'its ... row' refers to _processors.py's row (the antecedent is '_processors.py'), not to the _processors_keys.py row described in the DEVIATION section.
ABSENCE: no AC bullet verifies grep/absence of _processors_keys.py in docs/architecture.md.
findings.finding: The plan body explicitly states 'The sub-floor _processors_keys.py row is stale/superseded and is DELETED by this ticket' as a required deliverable, but no acceptance criterion verifies that this row has been removed from docs/architecture.md. The AC only covers deleting '_processors.py offenders-table row', leaving the _processors_keys.py deletion unchecked.
findings.location: DEVIATION section ('sub-floor _processors_keys.py row is stale/superseded and is DELETED by this ticket') vs. AC bullet ('its docs/architecture.md offenders-table row deleted')
scenarios: An implementer deletes only the _processors.py offenders row and misses the _processors_keys.py stale row; CI passes all stated ACs; the stale row lingers in docs/architecture.md causing future confusion about the intended seam.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC bullet: '…; the stale _processors_keys.py row in docs/architecture.md is also deleted (grep -c _processors_keys docs/architecture.md returns 0).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a row or note to docs/architecture.md recording `_processors_identity.py` as the adopted identity/attestation leaf seam, including the rationale that the attestation pair (attestation_kind + process_signature + _fold_author_attribution) was co-located with the keys cluster to clear the 100-LOC floor and avoid a back-edge cycle.
evidence: Plan states: 'The sub-floor _processors_keys.py row is stale/superseded and is DELETED by this ticket' — deletion is specified but no corresponding addition is specified.

Plan states: 'DEVIATION from the docs/architecture.md offenders row: that row names the seam _processors_keys.py' — this is a design decision that diverges from the documented plan; no persistent capture of the new decision is specified.
T7(b): a stale reference is removed but the replacement architectural fact is not written down anywhere persistent.
T5e(c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update' — the seam rename and scope-widening decision is not captured in any named persistent artifact.
findings.finding: The plan instructs deleting the docs/architecture.md offenders-table row for _processors_keys.py (and the _processors.py row) but does NOT specify adding any replacement entry or note in architecture.md recording the adopted seam (_processors_identity.py) and the design rationale (attestation pair co-located with keys cluster to clear the 100-LOC floor, renamed from _processors_keys.py). After execution, architecture.md will be silent about _processors_identity.py, leaving future maintainers without a persistent record of the widened-seam decision or where to find the identity/attestation cluster.
findings.location: AC bullet 4: '_processors.py removed from .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt; its docs/architecture.md offenders-table row deleted.' / Plan section 'DEVIATION from the docs/architecture.md offenders row'
scenarios: A future developer searching architecture.md for guidance on where cryptographic-identity logic lives finds no entry and may not discover _processors_identity.py without grepping the codebase.

A future module-size refactor ticket re-proposes splitting _processors_identity.py at the wrong seam because the rationale (attestation pair must travel with keys cluster to avoid back-edge) is only in the ticket, not in architecture.md.
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC (bullet 4) or a new bullet, add: 'docs/architecture.md offenders-table row for _processors_keys.py deleted AND a new row/note added documenting _processors_identity.py as the adopted seam (100-800 LOC, contains: _fold_author_attribution, _rederive_keyring_keys, process_key_event, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, attestation_kind, process_signature; rationale: attestation pair travels with keys cluster to clear 100-LOC floor and prevent back-edge cycle).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `_fold_author_attribution` is also called by `process_create` (line 150), `process_comment` (line 394), and `process_revert` (line 520) — all staying in `_processors.py` — so the `# noqa: F401` re-export doubles as the internal import; explicitly state that `_processors.py` must add `from ._processors_identity import _fold_author_attribution  # noqa: F401` (serving both external callers AND the three remaining internal callers), and retract the 'true leaf — references only each other' claim.
evidence: _processors.py line 150: `_fold_author_attribution(state, event)` inside process_create (staying in _processors.py)
_processors.py line 394: `_fold_author_attribution(_entry, event)` inside process_comment (staying in _processors.py)
_processors.py line 520: `_fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event)` inside process_revert (staying in _processors.py)
Plan text: 'These reference only each other (a true leaf): process_key_event calls _rederive_keyring_keys; process_signature calls attestation_kind and _fold_author_attribution'
Plan text: '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms'
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the 6 moved symbols 'reference only each other (a true leaf)' and that '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms.' This is contradicted by the code: _fold_author_attribution (lines 20-32 of _processors.py) is also called by process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520) — three functions that STAY in _processors.py. Moving _fold_author_attribution to the new leaf means _processors.py itself must import it back for internal use by those three functions, not merely re-export it to external callers. The plan does not specify this internal-import-back requirement, leaving an implementation gap: if _processors.py only adds `from ._processors_identity import _fold_author_attribution  # noqa: F401` as a re-export and nothing else changes, the three staying functions' calls to _fold_author_attribution will still resolve (Python will find it via the import), but the plan's 'true leaf — references only each other' framing is incorrect and may mislead the implementor into not adding the internal import, causing NameError at runtime.
findings.location: Plan body: 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' and '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms'; also _processors.py lines 150, 394, 520
scenarios: Implementor reads 'true leaf — references only each other' and adds only the re-export line to _processors.py; process_create / process_comment / process_revert fail at runtime with NameError if _fold_author_attribution is removed from _processors.py's local namespace without an explicit import-back.

The '# noqa: F401' re-export pattern is added but the symbol is accessed internally before the import — depending on import ordering at module load time, this could silently work or fail depending on Python's name resolution for intra-module calls.
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan body, replace 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' with: 'The 6 symbols are moved as a cluster; note _fold_author_attribution is also called by process_create, process_comment, and process_revert (staying in _processors.py), so the re-export line `from ._processors_identity import _fold_author_attribution  # noqa: F401` in _processors.py serves both external callers and those three internal callers — no separate import is needed beyond the re-export.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan that `_processors.py` must import `_fold_author_attribution` and `_bootstrap_genesis_keyring` from `_processors_identity.py` for its own INTERNAL call sites (process_create line 150, process_comment line 394, process_revert line 520, process_snapshot line 852) — not only as # noqa: F401 re-exports for external callers.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:150: _fold_author_attribution(state, event)  [inside process_create, which stays in _processors.py]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:394: _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event)  [inside process_comment, which stays in _processors.py]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:520: _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event)  [inside process_revert, which stays in _processors.py]
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:852: _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, ...)  [inside process_snapshot, which stays in _processors.py]
Plan says re-export is so 'rebar.reducer._processors.<symbol> keeps resolving for the reducer dispatch table' — omits internal call sites in _processors.py itself
findings.finding: _fold_author_attribution and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring are called by processors that STAY in _processors.py (process_create at line 150, process_comment at line 394, process_revert at line 520, and process_snapshot at line 852), yet the plan frames the re-import as serving only external callers. The plan must make explicit that _processors.py will also import these moved symbols for its own INTERNAL call sites — not just re-export them — to avoid an implementer accidentally leaving those internal call sites unresolved.
findings.location: Plan body: 'MOVE (by name)' and '_processors.py imports them back and re-exports them (# noqa: F401)'
scenarios: An implementer adds only a `# noqa: F401` re-export line but does not also use the imported name at the internal call sites (leaving dangling NameError calls), since the plan's framing suggests the re-export is purely for external consumers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Note: _processors.py also calls _fold_author_attribution internally (process_create, process_comment, process_revert) and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring (process_create, process_snapshot); the from ._processors_identity import ... line serves both internal callers and the re-export surface — no separate import needed.'
